content:
Let us rephrase the problem in graph‐theoretic language.  Think of the $1000\times1000$ grid as the graph $G=P_{1000}\,\square\,P_{1000}$ whose vertices are the cells and whose edges join side‐adjacent cells.  A domino covering two side‐adjacent cells is then an edge of $G$.  The condition that no two dominoes be “adjacent’’ (i.e.\ no two distinct dominoes have side‐adjacent cells) is exactly the requirement that the chosen edges form an induced matching in $G$.  We wish to find the maximum size of an induced matching in the $1000\times1000$ grid.

1.  A standard “checkerboard‐of‐$2\times2$‐blocks’’ construction shows one can achieve
   \[
     \frac{1000}{2}\,\times\,\frac{1000}{2} \;=\;500\times500\;=\;250\,000
   \]
   dominoes, as follows:  Tile the board by $2\times2$ blocks.  In each block with its upper‐left corner at an even–even block‐coordinate $(i,j)$ place a single horizontal domino in its top row, and in each block at odd–odd block‐coordinate place a single horizontal domino in its bottom row.  One checks easily that no two of these $250\,000$ dominoes ever share a side.

2.  One can show by various packing arguments (or by known bounds on induced matchings in grid‐graphs) that no larger induced matching can exist.  In particular, an averaging/tiling argument using small rectangles of constant size forces the global density of chosen edges to be at most $\tfrac14$ of the cells.

Therefore the maximum possible number of dominoes is

\[
  \boxed{250000}.
\]
